claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 18 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 18 and in approved education
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child receives qualifying benefits (e.g. UC, ESA) in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child receives qualifying benefits (e.g. UC, ESA) in their own right
circumstances: Child is 18 and in approved education
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child receives qualifying benefits (e.g. UC, ESA) in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me first review the official eligibility rules, then I'll ask you some questions about your circumstances.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for waiting. To help me assess your eligibility for Child Benefit, I need to ask you some questions about your circumstances.

First question: **Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you asking about?** (Please also let me know their names or how you'd like to refer to them, and their ages.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I'd like to find out if I'm eligible for Child Benefit. I have one child - my son Alex. He's 18 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I can see you have one child, Alex, who is 18 years old.

Since Alex is over 16, his eligibility for Child Benefit depends on whether he's in approved education or training. 

**Is Alex currently in approved education or training?** (For example, is he in full-time non-advanced education, such as studying for A-levels, or doing an approved training course?)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, he is. Alex is studying for his A-levels at sixth form college.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's helpful, thank you. Since Alex is 18 and in approved education (studying A-levels), that's relevant to your eligibility.

Now I need to understand your living arrangements with Alex. **Does Alex live with you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, he does. Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for confirming that. Now I need to ask about your residency status.

**Do you live in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - in Manchester.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, thank you for confirming that. Just a couple more questions to complete the assessment.

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?** (For example, Alex's other parent or another person who is responsible for him?)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. One final question to complete my assessment:

**Has Alex started any paid work for 24 hours or more per week, or is he receiving any benefits in his own right** (such as Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, he doesn't work - no job at all. But he does get Universal Credit in his own name.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. I now have enough details to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Based on the information you've given me and the official government rules, I need to inform you of the outcome:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Assessment Complete**

I'm sorry to inform you that **you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex**.

Here's why: While Alex meets several of the eligibility criteria (he's under 20, in approved education studying A-levels, lives with you in the UK, and no one else is claiming for him), there is one important rule that affects his eligibility.

According to the government rules, Child Benefit stops when a child starts receiving certain benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit. Since Alex is receiving Universal Credit in his own name, this disqualifies him from Child Benefit eligibility, even though he's still in approved education.

If you have any questions about this decision or if Alex's circumstances change in the future (for example, if he stops receiving Universal Credit), you may wish to contact the Child Benefit Office to discuss your situation further.

reasonings: Alex is 18 years old and studying A-levels at sixth form college, which would normally qualify for Child Benefit as he is under 20 and in approved education. He lives with you in the UK, and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for him. However, according to the government eligibility rules, you stop getting Child Benefit if your child starts getting certain benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit. Since Alex is receiving Universal Credit in his own name, this disqualifies him from Child Benefit eligibility, even though he is still in approved education.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (18 years old), who lives with them in the UK and is studying A-levels. While Alex meets most criteria for Child Benefit (under 20, in approved education, living with parent, no other claimant), he is receiving Universal Credit in his own right. Under UK Child Benefit rules, eligibility ends when a child starts receiving certain benefits such as Universal Credit, regardless of their education status. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

He lives with you in the UK, and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for him. However, according to the government eligibility rules, you stop getting Child Benefit if your child starts getting certain benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit. Since Alex is receiving Universal Credit in his own name, this disqualifies him from Child Benefit eligibility, even though he is still in approved education.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (18 years old), who lives with them in the UK and is studying A-levels. While Alex meets most criteria for Child Benefit (under 20, in approved education, living with parent, no other claimant), he is receiving Universal Credit in his own right. Under UK Child Benefit rules, eligibility ends when a child starts receiving certain benefits such as Universal Credit, regardless of their education status. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
